g.data-narration: Introduction to Operator Overloading using Friend Functions in C++
Operator overloading redefines C++ operators to work with user-defined data types like classes and structs
g.text: Redefining existing C++ operators to work with user-defined data types (classes/structs).
Enables intuitive syntax like c1 + c2 instead of c1.add(c2)
Learning Objective: Understand the concept and motivation behind operator overloading

g.data-narration: A friend function is a non-member function declared with the friend keyword inside a class
g.text: A non-member function declared with friend keyword inside a class.
Grants privileged access to private and protected members without being a member.
Key: Friend functions break encapsulation but provide flexibility for operator overloading

g.data-narration: The friend keyword is used inside the class to declare the operator overload function
g.text: friend Complex operator+(const Complex&, const Complex&);

g.data-narration: Friend functions enable commutative operations, direct private access, symmetry, and natural global form
Important rules: only existing operators, at least one user-defined operand, some operators cannot be overloaded

g.text: Only existing operators can be overloaded
At least one operand must be user-defined

g.data-narration: Example: Binary operator overloading for Complex numbers with friend function
g.text: Example 1: Binary Operator (+) for Complex Numbers

g.data-narration: Friend functions enable commutative operations like int + object that member functions cannot support
g.text: Friend functions solve the problem where member functions fail: int + object doesn't work with member functions!
Complex operator+(const Complex& c, int v) {
Complex operator+(int v, const Complex& c) {
This is why friend functions are essential for commutative operators!

g.data-narration: Example of comparison operator overloading for Distance class using friend function
g.text: friend bool operator>(const Distance&, const Distance&);
Return type is bool for comparison operators. Can also overload ==, !=, <, >=, <=

g.data-narration: Example of unary operator overloading including prefix and postfix increment for Counter class
Key difference: prefix increments first and returns reference, postfix returns copy with dummy int parameter
g.text: Prefix vs Postfix - The Key Difference
Returns copy, needs dummy int parameter
Note: The dummy int parameter in postfix is only for signature distinction - no value passed!

g.data-narration: Member functions use c1.operator+(c2) while friend functions use operator+(c1,c2) with full flexibility
g.text: Only 1 parameter; left operand must be class object
2 parameters; both operands explicit; supports int + obj

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: friend declaration inside class, supports commutativity, provides private access, but some operators must be members
university exam tips: remember syntax, commutativity questions, and function call form for success
g.text: Remember: Practice the Complex number example - it's the most frequently asked in university exams!
